coredata.dc:title: The beneficial impact of MXene on the electrochemical performance of graphene quantum dots for dopamine detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Facure, Murilo H.M.
Sampaio, Bruno S.
Mercante, Luiza A.
Correa, Daniel S.
coredata.dc:description: Considerable advancements in nanocomposite design have enabled the development of electrochemical sensors with promising features such as remarkable electrocatalytic activity, high electrical conductivity, and effective surface area. For instance, designing nanocomposites using 2D MXenes and 0D nanomaterials can be beneficial to combine the advantages of both individual constituents in order to enhance the device’s final performance. Herein, we developed a nanocomposite based on Ti3C2 MXene to tailor the electrochemical properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for sensing applications. Specifically, the electrochemical properties of different Ti3C2:GQDs ratios were evaluated by cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. Under optimized conditions, the nanocomposite-based sensor showed a linear response to dopamine (DA) in the concentration range of 40 – 400 μM and a limit of detection of 1.8 μM. Moreover, real sample analysis indicated that spiked DA could be determined accurately by Ti3C2/GQDs/GCE in human urine and sweat samples. The improved sensing performance was attributed to the combined effect of the large surface area of GQDs and the electrical conductivity of MXene, which approach can also be extended to develop other electrochemical sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
MXene
Electrochemical sensor
Dopamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Modification of electrodes and electrochemical measurements

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) HR-TEM images of GQDs. The inset in (a) shows a HR-TEM image of a single GQD.
(a) UV-Vis spectra of GQDs and Ti3C2, (b) FTIR, and (c) high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s of GQDs.

(a) Cyclic voltammograms and (b) Nyquist plots of GCE, GQDs, Ti3C2, Ti3C2:GQDs (1:3), Ti3C2:GQDs (1:1), and Ti3C2:GQDs (3:1) in a 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution with 0.1 M KCl. The inset in panel (b) shows a close view of the Nyquist plots.
(a and c) DPV response for different DA concentrations and (b and d) the corresponding linear regression curves obtained for GQDs/GCE and Ti3C2:GQDs(1:3)/GCE, respectively.
(a) CV curves of 100 μM DA using the Ti3C2:GQDs(1:3)/GCE at different scan rates. (b) Linear relationships of the Ipa and Ipc with the square root of the scan rate for DA.

(a) Repeatability test of the Ti3C2:GQDs(1:3)/GCE for four consecutive DPV measurements using the same electrode in a 100 μM DA solution and (b) the response for DA (100 μM) and possible interferents (500 μM).
Comparison of the performance of different carbon dots and graphene quantum dots-based sensors for dopamine detection.
Determination of DA in urine and sweat samples using the Ti3C2:GQDs(1:3) modified-electrode.

head.ce:title: The beneficial impact of MXene on the electrochemical performance of graphene quantum dots for dopamine detection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanotechnology National Laboratory for Agriculture (LNNA), Embrapa Instrumentação

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Considerable advancements in nanocomposite design have enabled the development of electrochemical sensors with promising features such as remarkable electrocatalytic activity, high electrical conductivity, and effective surface area. For instance, designing nanocomposites using 2D MXenes and 0D nanomaterials can be beneficial to combine the advantages of both individual constituents in order to enhance the device’s final performance. Herein, we developed a nanocomposite based on Ti3C2 MXene to tailor the electrochemical properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for sensing applications. Specifically, the electrochemical properties of different Ti3C2:GQDs ratios were evaluated by cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. Under optimized conditions, the nanocomposite-based sensor showed a linear response to dopamine (DA) in the concentration range of 40 – 400 μM and a limit of detection of 1.8 μM. Moreover, real sample analysis indicated that spiked DA could be determined accurately by Ti3C2/GQDs/GCE in human urine and sweat samples. The improved sensing performance was attributed to the combined effect of the large surface area of GQDs and the electrical conductivity of MXene, which approach can also be extended to develop other electrochemical sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a type of zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterial, have been widely explored in various applications, such as catalysis, energy storage, light-emitting diodes, sensing, and bioimaging due to their excellent aqueous dispersibility, biocompatibility, chemical stability, and ease of preparation from various carbon sources [1–3]. Besides, the application of these nanomaterials in electrochemical sensing has increased significantly recently due to their large specific surface area and multiple recognition sites [4–7]. However, in some cases, the electrochemical performance of GQDs alone is not ideal due to their low electrical conductivity [8]. This shortcoming can be circumvented by combining GQDs with varied conductive materials, including carbon nanotubes [9,10], reduced graphene oxide [11], conductive polymers [12], and metal/metal oxide nanomaterials [13–15].

2D transition metal carbides/nitrides (MXenes) have recently been proposed for electrochemical sensors due to their excellent electrical conductivity, tunable surface functional groups, and ease of processing [16–20]. However, the research on the sensing properties of MXenes is still in its initial stage, which opens the possibility of developing new electrochemical sensing platforms. The combination of MXenes with graphene-based materials has been extensively explored in the scientific literature [21,22]. However, the integration of GQDs with MXenes remains relatively under investigated, particularly aiming at optimizing their electrochemical performance. Therefore, improving the efficiency of electrochemical sensors using such materials is highly attractive, as electrochemical sensors can be cost-effective and present advantages such as high sensitivity, rapid response, and portability, making them ideal for in situ applications [23,24].

In this context, here we investigated the influence of MXene on the electrochemical properties of GQDs for sensing applications. Specifically, the GQDs-modified electrode, namely Ti3C2:GQDs (1:3) nanocomposite, showed enhanced electrochemical performance due to the high active surface area of the GQDs and the high conductivity of MXene. Under optimal conditions, the Ti3C2:GQDs(1:3)-modified electrode showed good linearity and selectivity for the detection of dopamine (DA), an important catecholamine neurotransmitter. Meanwhile, the nanocomposite was successfully employed in the electrochemical detection of DA in biological fluids (urine and sweat).

The Ti3AlC2 MAX phase was obtained from Carbon-Ukraine, Ltd. Hydrofluoric acid (HF, 48.5–51 %) and lithium chloride (LiCl, 99 %) were obtained from Acros Organics. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36.5–38 %) was purchased from Fisher Scientific. β-D-Glucose (≥ 80 %) used in the GQDs synthesis was obtained from MP Biomedicals. Potassium hydroxide (KOH, ≥85 %), potassium hexacyanoferrate(III) (K3Fe(CN)6, ≥ 99.0 %), potassium hexacyanoferrate(II) trihydrate (K4Fe(CN)6·3 H2O, 98.5–102.0 %), potassium phosphate monobasic anhydrous (KH2PO4, ≥ 99.0 %), potassium phosphate dibasic anhydrous (K2HPO4, ≥ 98.0 %) and dopamine hydrochloride were all purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Ascorbic acid (AA, Fisher Scientific), β-D-Glucose (Glu), uric acid (UA, ≥ 99 %, Sigma-Aldrich), Urea (99.0–100.5 %, Sigma-Aldrich), potassium chloride (KCl, 99.0–100.5 %, Sigma-Aldrich), sodium chloride (NaCl, ≥ 99.5 %, Sigma-Aldrich), calcium chloride (CaCl2, ≥99.9 %, Sigma-Aldrich), zinc chloride (ZnCl2, ≥ 98 %, Sigma-Aldrich), and magnesium chloride (MgCl2, ≥ 98 %, Sigma-Aldrich) were used as interferents.

The synthesis of Ti3C2 MXene was performed following the methodology described in [25,26]. Initially, 6 mL of deionized (DI) water was introduced into a high-density polyethylene (HDPE) bottle, followed by the addition of 12 mL of HCl and 2 mL of hydrofluoric acid (HF). Subsequently, 1 g of MAX phase precursor was gradually added to the solution, which was stirred at 35 ºC for 24 h. Centrifugation cycles (3500 rpm for 5 min) were employed to wash the multilayered MXene until achieving a pH ≥ 6. The obtained MXene slurry was redispersed in 50 mL of a 0.5 M LiCl solution and stirred for an additional 24 h at 35 ºC. The MXene was separated via centrifugation. The supernatant was discarded until it started to exhibit a black color, indicating the presence of delaminated Ti3C2 MXene.

The GQDs synthesis was adapted from [27]. Initially, 6 g of β-D-glucose was added to a 250 mL beaker, which was then placed in an oil bath at 180°C. The solution color changed from colorless to orange, indicating the successful GQDs formation. After 10 min, the system was removed from the oil bath, and 25 mL of DI water was added under vigorous stirring. The obtained dispersion was dialyzed using a 2 kDa membrane to remove residual glucose. The GQDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying the dialyzed dispersion.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were obtained using a JEOL 2100 F microscope. The samples were prepared by drop-casting the dispersion on a lacey carbon-coated Cu grid. The UV-Vis spectra of the materials were obtained using a UV-Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Evolution 201, ThermoScientific). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy analyses were carried out with the GQDs powder. The FTIR spectra were obtained using the ATR mode, collecting 64 scans from 4000 to 400 cm−1 at a 4 cm−1 resolution in a Bruker Vertex 70 equipment. The XPS measurements were performed in a PHI VersaProbe 5000 instrument (Physical Electronics) using a 100 μm and 25 W monochromatic Al-Kα (1486.6 eV) X-ray source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification of electrodes and electrochemical measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Dispersions of Ti3C2 and GQDs (2 mg/mL) were prepared and mixed in different ratios (1:3, 1:1, and 3:1) to obtain Ti3C2/GQDs dispersions with a final concentration of 0.5 mg/mL. Before modifying the electrode's surface, the GCEs were activated using a 0.1 M KOH solution. 20 µL of the Ti3C2/GQDs dispersions were employed to modify the GCEs by drop-casting, ensuring a complete coverage of the GCE surface. The same procedure was used with Ti3C2 and GQDs dispersions. The electrodes were left to dry overnight at room temperature.

The electrochemical measurements were performed in 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (pH = 6) using a three-electrode cell and a VMP3 potentiostat (BioLogic). To validate the sensor for real applications, a pH of 6 was chosen because it is close to the pH of human sweat and urine [28,29]. The modified GCEs were used as working electrodes, Ag/AgCl as reference electrode, and platinum (Pt) foil as counter electrode. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed from 0 to 0.45 V at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed in a 5 mM [Fe(CN)₆]³ ⁻/⁴⁻ solution containing 0.1 M KCl at open circuit potential (OCP) over a frequency range from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz, with an applied amplitude of 10 mV. The DA detection experiments were carried out using the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) technique in the range from 0.1 to 0.5 V, using an amplitude of 25 mV, pulse width of 0.25 s, and pulse period of 0.6 s.

A typical TEM image of GQDs displayed in Fig. 1a demonstrated the formation of quantum dots with an average diameter of 3.5 nm. HR-TEM image (inset of Fig. 1a) shows that the GQDs are polycrystalline with a lattice parameter of 0.24 nm, which corresponds to the (100) lattice fringes of graphene [30], while the characteristic sp2 hybridized honeycomb structure of the GQDs can be visualized in Fig. 1b [31,32].

The typical absorption profile of the GQDs ( Fig. 2a) exhibits a peak at 230 nm related to the π–π* of the CC bonds on the GQDs core, while the peaks from 250 to 350 nm can be ascribed to the n–π* transitions from the different oxygen functional groups present on the GQDs surface [33]. Fig. 2a also shows the Ti3C2 UV-Vis spectrum, in which characteristic peaks at 265 nm and 320 nm, and a plasmonic peak centered at 770 nm confirm the successful Ti3C2 MXene synthesis [25,26].

FTIR and XPS spectroscopy were employed to obtain information about the oxygen functionalities present within the GQDs structure. As shown in Fig. 2b, the FTIR spectrum shows a broad band at around 3300 cm−1 associated with the stretching vibration of O–H bond, while the peak at 2910 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1 are attributed to the C–H stretching vibration and the CC bond [13,34]. Other peaks related to oxygen-containing groups can be found at 1425 (O–H), 1350 (C–O), 1005 (C–OH), and 790 cm–1 (C–O–C) [13,34]. Fig. 2c shows the high-resolution XPS spectrum fitted for C 1 s. As can be noted, the main component (67.8 % peak area) appearing at 283.8 eV is related to graphitic sp2 (CC) carbon, and the peak at 282.3 eV is assigned to the sp3 carbon (C-C and C-H). The remaining XPS peaks are related to C–O (285.2 eV) and CO (286.1 eV) oxygen-based functional groups [35]. The presence of O–H groups on the GQDs structure can specifically interact with the amino groups of dopamine [36], enhancing the possibility of redox reactions on the electrode surface.

The influence of different Ti3C2 concentrations on the electrochemical performance of GQDs/GCE electrode response was evaluated with CV and EIS measurements in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. As shown in Fig. 3a, modifying the GCE surface with GQDs led to a discernible reduction in the peak current, which could be related to the repulsion between the oxygen-containing groups of GQDs and the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− anions [37]. With the incorporation of Ti3C2 at the ratio of 1:3 (Ti3C2:GQDs), the peak current increased, and the oxidation peak shifted to lower potentials due to the excellent electrical conductivity of the MXene, which improves the electron transfer at the electrode surface [19,38]. However, lower redox peak currents are observed for high amounts of Ti3C2. This, in turn, may be caused by the MXene capacitive behavior and the aggregation of the material. The CV profiles of the Ti3C2 and the Ti3C2:GQDs(3:1) present a rectangular shape typical of a double-layer capacitor-like response [39]. As the amount of GQDs increases, the capacitive behavior no longer dominates the response, and redox peaks become more prominent due to the high electrochemical activity of the GQDs [39,40]. Also, the aggregation of the MXene may play a role in decreasing the active surface area of the electrode. These results indicated that at the Ti3C2:GQDs(1:3) ratio, the GQDs act as interlayer spacers, which could effectively suppress the stacking of the MXene sheets [41].

Fig. 3b shows the impedance plots as Nyquist plots for the bare and modified electrodes. The nearly parallel lines to the -Z’’ axis of the Ti3C2 and the Ti3C2:GQDs(3:1) plots confirm the pure capacitance characteristic of the MXene [42]. The semi-circle diameters at high frequencies correspond to the electron transfer resistance (Rct) at the surface of the electrode and were estimated by fitting it to the Randles circuit [43]. As can be noted, the Rct of the Ti3C2:GQDs(1:3) modified electrode is smaller than that of all other electrodes, which is consistent with the CV measurements. The unmodified GCE exhibited a Rct value of 990 Ω, while in contrast, for the Ti3C2:GQDs(1:3)/GCE, the value of Rct was reduced to 780 Ω, suggesting that the nanocomposite enhanced the electron transfer ability between the redox probe and the electrode surface.

The electrochemical characterization demonstrates that combining GQDs with an optimal amount of MXene is advantageous for achieving high electric conductivity coupled with enhanced electrochemical activity, provided mainly by MXene and GQDs, respectively.

The performance of the Ti3C2:GQDs(1:3) modified electrode for DA detection was investigated and compared with the GQDs/GCE. As shown in Fig. 4, the oxidation peak current gradually increased with the DA concentration for both electrodes. However, with respect to the value for GQDs/GCE, the peak current of the Ti3C2:GQDs(1:3) nanocomposite increases by ∼20 %, due to the higher electrical conductivity and increased electrochemical active surface area. Moreover, the nanocomposite allowed the detection of DA over a wide linear range from 20 to 400 µM, as shown in Fig. 4b. The linear regression equations for GQDs and Ti3C2:GQDs(1:3) are ΔI (µA) = 0.010 × DA (µM) + 1.205 (R2 = 0.997, n = 3) and ΔI (µA) = 0.012 × DA (µM) + 2.116 (R2 = 0.991, n = 3), respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated as 6.4 µM and 1.8 µM for GQDs and Ti3C2:GQDs(1:3), respectively. As shown in Table 1, the LOD of DA achieved with Ti3C2:GQDs(1:3) is competitive or even lower than other sensors reported in the literature based on carbon dots or graphene quantum dots.

To gain a deeper understanding of the detection mechanism, the effect of the scan rate on the Ti3C2:GQDs(1:3) modified electrode was evaluated ( Fig. 5(a-b)). As shown in Fig. 5b, both the anodic and cathodic peaks current increase linearly with the square root of scan rate (v 1/2), indicating that the electrochemical reaction of DA is a diffusion-controlled process [50]. Moreover, with increasing scan rate, the anodic peak potential shifted positively, while it shifted negatively for the cathodic peak potential, suggesting that the electrochemical oxidation and reduction of DA is a quasi-reversible behavior [13].

To verify the stability of the sensor for DA detection, repeatability was estimated by performing consecutive DPV measurements (n = 4) using the same electrode ( Fig. 6a). A relative standard deviation equal to 0.7 % was obtained, indicating the Ti3C2:GQDs(1:3) electrode’s stability for DA detection. The selectivity of the developed sensor towards a series of interferences such as ascorbic acid (AA), glucose (Glu), uric acid (UA), urea, and salts (KCl, NaCl, CaCl2, ZnCl2, MgCl2) was also investigated, as shown in Fig. 6b. The interferents were used in a concentration five times higher than that of DA. It can be observed that only AA presents interference in the detection of up to 25 %. However, the DA detection in the presence of all other interferences led to a relative response close to 100 %, demonstrating the high selectivity of the sensor towards DA detection.

The ability of the Ti3C2:GQDs(1:3) sensor to detect DA in real samples was also evaluated with recovery experiments. Table 2 depicts recovery values of 94.1 and 98.9 % for urine and sweat samples, respectively, indicating that the sensor is promising for analyzing body fluid samples.

The combination of GQDs with Ti3C2 MXene was investigated to improve the performance of an electrochemical sensor toward dopamine (DA) detection. Specifically, an optimal composition (Ti3C2:GQDs(1:3)) was achieved in which the electrode exhibited excellent performance mainly due to the electrochemical activity of GQDs and the electrical conductivity of MXene. Besides high sensitivity, the Ti3C2:GQDs(1:3) sensor could successfully detect DA in the presence of potential interferents and also in real samples (urine and sweat). These results demonstrate that combining carbon nanostructures with MXenes can enhance the final sensor performance by leveraging their conductive properties. This synergy enables the exploration of these composites in other electrochemical sensors for detecting varied analytes of biomedical or environmental interest in practical applications.

Bruno S. Sampaio: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Luiza A. Mercante: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Daniel S. Correa: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Murilo H.M. Facure: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support from FAPESP (2021/11683-8, 2017/10582-8, 2018/22214-6), CNPq (405819/2022-6), MCTI-Sis­Nano (CNPq/402.287/2013-4), Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior - Brasil (CAPES) - Código de Financiamento 001, and Rede Agronano (EMBRAPA). The authors thank Yury Gogotsi and Drexel University for providing the opportunity and the facilities to develop the work, Mark Anayee and Robert W. Lord for the XPS analysis and the TEM images.
